lessons.title: What a Random Process Is (and Stationarity)
lessons.content:
**Hook.** Turn an old radio between stations and you hear *hiss*. Take a photo in a dark room and it looks *grainy*. Stand in a crowd and you hear a *murmur*. None of these has an exact value you could predict ahead of time, yet each has a steady character you *can* describe. That is a **random process**: randomness that unfolds over time.

**Build.** In topic 10 you met a **random variable** — a single uncertain number, like one dice roll. A **random process** is the next step up: a whole *signal* (a value that changes over time) whose exact wiggles you can't predict, only describe statistically. Think of it as a fresh random variable at every instant, all strung together into a waveform.

A helpful picture: imagine recording that radio hiss on a thousand identical radios at once. Each recording (called a **realization** or **sample function**) looks different in detail, but they all share the same *statistics* — the same average loudness, the same kind of fizz. The whole stack of possible recordings is the **ensemble**.

**Noise** is the everyday name for unwanted random fluctuation riding on top of something you care about: the hiss on the radio voice, the grain on the photo, the jitter on a temperature reading that drifts around its true average.

The most useful simplifying idea is **stationarity**: the statistics don't change over time. The hiss sounds the same now as it did a minute ago — same average, same spread. A process whose average and spread stay put is called **stationary**. (Warming-up electronics or a fading signal break this — their statistics drift.)

**Recap.** A random process is a signal you can only describe statistically; noise is unwanted random fluctuation; stationary means “the statistics stay the same over time.”

items.prompt: In plain terms, what is a random process?
choices: A single fixed number that never changes
A signal (a value that changes over time) whose exact values you can't predict, only describe statistically
A guaranteed-repeatable waveform you can write down with one formula
A list of frequencies with no time information
items.explanation: A random process is randomness that unfolds over time — a signal whose moment-to-moment values are unpredictable, but whose overall statistics (like average and spread) you can describe. Choice A is just a constant; a single fixed number is not random and has no time variation. A fully predictable waveform (C) is deterministic, not random.

items.prompt: How does a random process relate to a random variable from the previous topic?
choices: They are exactly the same thing with different names
A random process is like a fresh random variable at every instant of time, strung together into a signal
A random variable is more complex than a random process
A random process has no randomness in it at all
items.explanation: A random variable is one uncertain number. A random process extends that idea across time: picture a random variable at each instant, linked together into a waveform. That is why everything you learned about averages and spread still applies — now they can also depend on time.

items.prompt: Which everyday thing is the best example of “noise” in the technical sense?
choices: A perfectly clean tone from a tuning fork
The static hiss you hear between radio stations
A printed page of text
A battery's fixed voltage
items.explanation: Noise is unwanted random fluctuation. Radio static is exactly that — an unpredictable hiss riding on (or replacing) the signal you wanted. A pure tuning-fork tone and a fixed battery voltage are clean, predictable, and not random; printed text is information, not a random fluctuation.

items.prompt: What is a single “realization” (sample function) of a random process?
choices: The complete set of every possible waveform the process could produce
One particular recorded waveform — just one of the many the process could have produced
The average value across all recordings
The frequency content of the process
items.explanation: A realization is one actual recorded trace — the one that nature happened to produce this time. The complete collection of all possible traces (choice A) is the ensemble, which is a different idea. The average (C) and frequency content (D) are summaries, not individual waveforms.

items.prompt: Imagine recording the same radio hiss on a thousand identical radios at once. That whole stack of recordings is called the:
choices: Ensemble
Spectrum
Bandwidth
Threshold
items.explanation: The ensemble is the collection of all possible realizations — here, the full stack of a thousand recordings. They differ in detail but share the same statistics. Spectrum and bandwidth describe frequency content, and a threshold is a decision level; none of those names the stack of recordings.

items.prompt: A process is called STATIONARY when:
choices: Its waveform never moves or changes at all
Its statistics (like average and spread) stay the same over time
It has zero average value
It only exists at one instant in time
items.explanation: Stationary means the statistical description doesn't drift with time: the hiss has the same average loudness and spread now as a minute ago. It does NOT mean the waveform is frozen (choice A) — the signal still wiggles every instant; it's the *statistics* that hold steady. A zero average (C) is neither required nor sufficient.

items.prompt: Which situation would BREAK stationarity (make the statistics change over time)?
choices: Steady radio hiss that sounds identical minute after minute
A receiver that is warming up, so its noise level slowly climbs
A constant temperature reading with the same small wiggle all day
A crowd murmur that keeps the same average loudness
items.explanation: If a receiver warms up, its noise power slowly rises — the average and spread are changing with time, so the process is non-stationary. The other three all keep their statistics steady over time, which is exactly what stationary means.

items.prompt: Why do engineers care so much about noise being random rather than just “bad”?
choices: Because random things can never be measured at all
Because if we can describe its statistics, we can predict its average effect and design ways to fight it
Because randomness means the noise will eventually disappear on its own
Because random noise carries the useful information we want
items.explanation: Even though we can't predict noise instant by instant, treating it as a random process lets us pin down its statistics — average power, spread, how it spreads over frequency — and that is exactly what lets us design filters and detectors to push past it. Noise doesn't vanish on its own (C), and it is the *unwanted* part, not the information (D).

items.prompt: A grainy photo taken in low light is a good everyday picture of noise because:
choices: The grain is a deliberate artistic pattern with a fixed shape
The random speckle is unwanted fluctuation sitting on top of the true image
Low light removes all randomness from the sensor
The grain only appears in bright daylight
items.explanation: In low light the camera sensor catches very little real signal, so random speckle (noise) becomes visible riding on top of the true picture — exactly the “unwanted random fluctuation on a signal” definition. The grain isn't a fixed designed pattern, and low light makes the randomness *more* noticeable, not less.

items.prompt: Which statement best captures the difference between a deterministic signal and a random process?
choices: A deterministic signal is predictable from a formula; a random process can only be described statistically
A deterministic signal is always louder than a random process
A random process has a formula but a deterministic signal does not
There is no difference; the terms mean the same thing
items.explanation: A deterministic signal (like a clean sine wave) can be written down exactly with a formula and reproduced perfectly. A random process can't — you can only state its statistics. Loudness (B) is unrelated, and the formula situation in (C) is backwards.

lessons.title: Averages, Ergodicity, and Correlation
lessons.content:
**Hook.** If hiss is unpredictable instant by instant, how can we measure it? We *average*. And once we ask “does the signal now resemble the signal a moment ago?” we are measuring **correlation** — the key to telling structure from pure randomness.

**Build.** There are two ways to average a random process. An **ensemble average** averages *across* many recordings at one chosen instant — line up your thousand radio recordings and average the value each shows at, say, the 3-second mark. A **time average** averages *along* one recording over a long stretch of time. The catch: in real life you usually have just one recording. **Ergodicity** is the happy property that says, for many well-behaved stationary processes, the time average from a single long recording equals the ensemble average. In words: *one long recording can stand in for the whole crowd.* That is why measuring the hiss power off one trace is legitimate.

Next, **autocorrelation**, written $R_{xx}(\\tau)$. It answers: how similar, on average, is the signal to a copy of itself shifted by a time lag $\\tau$? At zero lag ($\\tau = 0$) you're comparing the signal with itself, so $R_{xx}(0)$ is as big as it gets — it equals the **average power** of the process. As the lag grows, the similarity usually fades. For pure noise it drops to nothing almost immediately: each instant is unrelated to the next.

**Cross-correlation**, $R_{xy}(\\tau)$, does the same but between *two different* signals — how well does signal X line up with a shifted copy of signal Y? Slide one past the other and the lag where they match best tells you the delay between them (the idea behind finding an echo).

**Recap.** Averaging tames randomness; ergodicity lets one recording represent the ensemble; autocorrelation measures self-similarity versus lag (peaking at zero lag = power); cross-correlation matches two signals to find their delay.

items.prompt: An ENSEMBLE average is taken by averaging:
choices: Along one recording over a long stretch of time
Across many recordings at one fixed instant of time
Over all the frequencies present in the signal
Over the colors of the noise
items.explanation: An ensemble average lines up all the realizations and averages them at a single chosen instant — a “vertical” average across the stack. Averaging along one recording over time (choice A) is the *time* average, a separate operation that only matches the ensemble average when the process is ergodic.

items.prompt: What does ergodicity let you do that is hugely practical?
choices: Predict the exact future value of the noise
Use one long recording to estimate statistics that would otherwise need the whole ensemble
Remove all noise from a measurement perfectly
Turn a random process into a deterministic one
items.explanation: Ergodicity says the time average of a single long recording equals the ensemble average — so one trace can stand in for the whole crowd of recordings you can't actually make. It does not let you predict exact future values, nor does it eliminate randomness; it just makes one recording statistically representative.

items.prompt: Autocorrelation $R_{xx}(\\tau)$ measures:
choices: How loud the signal is at a single instant
How similar, on average, the signal is to a copy of itself shifted by a lag $\\tau$
The probability that the signal is positive
The number of frequencies in the signal
items.explanation: Autocorrelation compares a signal with a time-shifted copy of itself and reports the average similarity as a function of the shift (lag) $\\tau$. It is about self-similarity versus lag, not the instantaneous loudness, a probability, or a frequency count.

items.prompt: Why is $R_{xx}(0)$ (autocorrelation at zero lag) special?
choices: It is always zero
It is the largest value of the autocorrelation and equals the average power of the process
It is the frequency of the signal
It is the smallest value the autocorrelation can take
items.explanation: At zero lag you compare the signal with itself with no shift, so the match is perfect and the value is the largest possible — and it works out to the process's average power (mean-square value). It is therefore a maximum, not zero (A) and not a minimum (D).

items.prompt: For pure random noise, how does the autocorrelation behave as the lag $\\tau$ grows away from zero?
choices: It stays high forever because noise is strongly self-similar
It drops to nearly zero almost immediately, because each instant is unrelated to the next
It grows without bound
It becomes negative and stays large
items.explanation: In pure noise, one instant tells you almost nothing about the next, so the self-similarity collapses to near zero as soon as you shift by any meaningful lag. That sharp peak at zero lag falling to nothing is the signature of white noise. Strong self-similarity at large lag (A) would mean the signal has structure, not pure noise.

items.prompt: Cross-correlation $R_{xy}(\\tau)$ differs from autocorrelation because it compares:
choices: A signal with a shifted copy of itself
Two different signals at a relative time shift
A signal with its own frequency spectrum
The noise power with the signal power
items.explanation: Cross-correlation slides one signal past a *different* signal and measures how well they line up at each lag. Autocorrelation (choice A) compares a signal with itself. Cross-correlation is what you use to find the delay between, say, a sent pulse and its echo.

items.prompt: You send out a sound and listen for its echo. Sliding the original past the recording and finding the lag of best match tells you:
choices: The color of the noise
The time delay to the reflecting object
The temperature of the room
The total power of the signal
items.explanation: The lag where the cross-correlation peaks is the time shift that makes the echo line up with the original — i.e. the round-trip delay. This is the core idea behind sonar and radar ranging. It says nothing directly about noise color, temperature, or power.

items.prompt: For a real-valued stationary process, the autocorrelation is an EVEN function, meaning:
choices: $R_{xx}(\\tau) = R_{xx}(-\\tau)$, so shifting left or right by the same amount gives the same value
$R_{xx}(\\tau)$ keeps growing as the lag grows
$R_{xx}(\\tau)$ is always negative
items.explanation: Self-similarity doesn't care which direction you shift: a lag of $+\\tau$ gives the same correlation as a lag of $-\\tau$, so $R_{xx}(\\tau) = R_{xx}(-\\tau)$. That is what “even” means. An odd relationship (A) would imply the value at zero lag is zero, which contradicts it being the peak power.

items.prompt: Why can't we just read a single instantaneous value to characterize noise, instead of averaging?
choices: Because a single value is random and tells you almost nothing about the overall behavior
Because instantaneous values are illegal to measure
Because averaging changes the noise into a signal
Because noise has no values at single instants
items.explanation: One instant of noise is just one draw of a random variable — it could be anything and tells you little. Averaging over time (or across the ensemble) smooths out the randomness and reveals the stable statistics, like average power. Averaging summarizes the noise; it doesn't convert it into a signal.

items.prompt: A process is stationary but you are told it is NOT ergodic. What does that mean in practice?
choices: Its statistics change over time
One long recording's time average may not equal the true ensemble average
It has no autocorrelation
It must be deterministic
items.explanation: Stationary means the statistics don't drift over time. Ergodic is the extra promise that a single recording's time average matches the ensemble average. If a stationary process is not ergodic, that promise fails — one trace can mislead you about the whole ensemble. Stationarity (A) is still intact; that's given.

lessons.title: Power Spectral Density and Types of Noise
lessons.content:
**Hook.** Hiss has a *color*. Deep rumble and high fizz are different even at the same loudness. The tool that captures this is the **power spectral density (PSD)** — it says how a signal's power is spread across frequency, just like a prism spreads light into colors.

**Build.** The PSD, written $S_{xx}(f)$, answers: how much of the noise's power sits at each frequency? Add up (integrate) the PSD over all frequencies and you get back the total power. The deep link between the two views you've met — time-domain autocorrelation and frequency-domain PSD — is the **Wiener–Khinchin theorem**: *the PSD is the Fourier transform of the autocorrelation.* In plain terms, the self-similarity-vs-lag picture and the power-vs-frequency picture are two sides of the same coin; knowing one gives you the other.

**White noise** is the simplest case: its PSD is *flat* — equal power at every frequency, just as white light mixes all colors equally. Its autocorrelation (from Wiener–Khinchin) is a single spike at zero lag: each instant is unrelated to the next. “White” is a statement about the *spectrum's shape*, not about loudness. **Colored noise** is anything with an uneven spectrum — more power in some frequency bands than others (a low rumble is “red,” a $1/f$ hiss is “pink”).

Where does noise come from? **Thermal (Johnson) noise** is the unavoidable hiss from the jiggling of electrons in any warm resistor. Its available power is famously $N = kTB$: Boltzmann's constant $k$ times absolute temperature $T$ (in kelvin) times bandwidth $B$ (in hertz). Hotter or wider-band means more noise. It's essentially flat over normal bandwidths, so it's treated as white. **AWGN** — Additive White Gaussian Noise — is the standard model: it *adds* to your signal, is *white* (flat spectrum), and has a *Gaussian* (bell-curve) amplitude. A device's **noise figure** is just a number (in dB) saying how much extra noise that device adds beyond the unavoidable minimum.

**Recap.** PSD shows power versus frequency; Wiener–Khinchin ties it to autocorrelation; white noise is flat, colored noise is uneven; thermal noise is $kTB$; AWGN (additive, white, Gaussian) is the workhorse model.

items.prompt: Power spectral density (PSD) tells you:
choices: The exact voltage of the signal at each instant
How the signal's power is spread across frequency
The probability that the signal is positive
The time delay between two signals
items.explanation: PSD is the power-versus-frequency picture: it shows how much power sits at each frequency, like a prism spreading light into colors. It is not an instant-by-instant voltage (that's the waveform), nor a probability, nor a delay.

items.prompt: The Wiener–Khinchin theorem says that the PSD is:
choices: The square of the average value of the signal
The Fourier transform of the autocorrelation function
Always flat for every signal
Unrelated to the autocorrelation
items.explanation: Wiener–Khinchin links the two views you've learned: the PSD (power vs frequency) is the Fourier transform of the autocorrelation (self-similarity vs lag). Knowing one gives the other. It is certainly related to autocorrelation (so D is wrong), and only white noise has a flat PSD (so C is wrong).

items.prompt: What makes noise “white”?
choices: Its amplitude follows a bell curve
Its power spectral density is flat — equal power at every frequency
It has no power at all
Its power is concentrated at one single frequency
items.explanation: “White” describes the *spectrum's shape*: flat, with equal power at every frequency, by analogy with white light mixing all colors. It is not a statement about the amplitude distribution (a Gaussian bell curve is a separate property), and flat is the opposite of power piled at one frequency.

items.prompt: What is the autocorrelation of ideal white noise, and why?
choices: A wide smooth hump, because nearby instants are strongly related
A single spike at zero lag and essentially zero elsewhere, because each instant is unrelated to the next
A flat constant line at all lags
A growing curve, because correlation increases with lag
items.explanation: A flat (white) PSD transforms, via Wiener–Khinchin, into a single spike at zero lag. That matches the meaning of white noise: every instant is independent of every other, so there's correlation only with itself (zero lag) and none elsewhere.

items.prompt: Colored noise differs from white noise because colored noise has:
choices: An uneven spectrum, with more power in some frequency bands than others
A perfectly flat spectrum
No randomness at all
Power only at zero frequency
items.explanation: “Colored” means the power-vs-frequency curve is uneven — some bands carry more power than others (a deep rumble is “red,” a $1/f$ hiss is “pink”). White noise is the special flat case. The word refers to the *shape* of the spectrum, not the loudness.

items.prompt: Thermal (Johnson) noise comes from:
choices: A deliberate signal injected by the transmitter
The random thermal jiggling of electrons in any warm resistor
Software rounding errors only
The signal bouncing off nearby objects
items.explanation: Thermal noise is the unavoidable hiss produced by electrons jiggling about due to heat in any resistive element above absolute zero. It is not injected on purpose, not a software artifact, and not a reflection (that would be an echo or clutter).

items.prompt: In the thermal-noise formula $N = kTB$, what do $T$ and $B$ represent?
choices: $T$ is time and $B$ is the number of bits
$T$ is absolute temperature (in kelvin) and $B$ is the bandwidth (in hertz)
$T$ is the threshold and $B$ is the battery voltage
$T$ is torque and $B$ is the magnetic field
items.explanation: In $N = kTB$, $k$ is Boltzmann's constant, $T$ is absolute temperature in kelvin, and $B$ is the bandwidth in hertz. So hotter ($T$ up) or wider-band ($B$ up) systems pick up more thermal noise power. The other readings of the letters don't fit the physics.

items.prompt: According to $N = kTB$, the thermal noise power goes UP if you:
choices: Cool the device down
Widen the bandwidth or raise the temperature
Narrow the bandwidth
Turn the signal off
items.explanation: Since $N = kTB$, noise power is directly proportional to both temperature $T$ and bandwidth $B$. Widening the band or heating the device increases $N$. Cooling it or narrowing the band reduces $N$ — which is exactly why sensitive receivers are sometimes cryogenically cooled and band-limited.

items.prompt: AWGN stands for Additive White Gaussian Noise. What does the “Additive” part mean?
choices: The noise multiplies the signal
The noise simply adds on top of the signal: received = signal + noise
The noise replaces the signal entirely
The noise only appears when you add more devices
items.explanation: “Additive” means the noise is laid on top of the signal by simple addition: what you receive is the clean signal plus a noise term. “White” means its spectrum is flat, and “Gaussian” means its amplitudes follow a bell curve. Multiplying or replacing the signal would be a different (non-additive) model.

items.prompt: Why is AWGN such a popular model for noise?
choices: Because it perfectly describes every possible interference
Because it captures thermal noise well and is simple to work with mathematically
Because it has no Gaussian part
Because it removes the need to measure anything
items.explanation: AWGN is the workhorse because thermal noise really is roughly additive, white, and Gaussian, and those three properties make the math tractable for designing detectors and predicting performance. It doesn't claim to model *every* impairment (like structured interference), and it certainly keeps its Gaussian character.

items.prompt: A device's NOISE FIGURE (in dB) tells you:
choices: How much extra noise the device adds beyond the unavoidable minimum
The exact temperature of the room in kelvin
The frequency of the strongest signal
How many bits the device can store
items.explanation: Noise figure is a single number summarizing how much a component degrades the signal-to-noise ratio — i.e. how much noise it adds on top of the minimum thermal noise. A perfect, noiseless device would have a noise figure of 0 dB. It is not a temperature, frequency, or storage measure.

lessons.title: SNR, Filtering Noise, and the Matched Filter
lessons.content:
**Hook.** A whisper is easy to hear in a quiet room and impossible at a rock concert. What matters isn't how loud the whisper is on its own — it's how far it stands *above the surrounding noise*. That ratio has a name: **signal-to-noise ratio (SNR)**.

**Build.** **SNR** is simply signal power divided by noise power, usually quoted in decibels. The crucial insight: turning up the volume amplifies the signal *and* the noise together, so the ratio doesn't change. Detection depends on SNR, not on absolute loudness. A bigger SNR means the signal sticks up more clearly above the noise floor and is easier to detect.

One way to raise SNR is **filtering**. A filter is a device that passes some frequencies and blocks others. If your signal lives in a narrow band but noise is spread everywhere (white), a filter that keeps only the signal's band throws away most of the noise while keeping the signal — SNR goes up. (A useful fact: white noise that has been filtered comes out *colored*, because the filter has reshaped its spectrum.)

The cleverest filter for pulling a *known* signal shape out of noise is the **matched filter**. Instead of blocking by frequency, it is shaped like the signal itself and effectively *correlates* the incoming data with a stored copy of the expected signal. When the real signal arrives and lines up with the template, the output spikes; noise, being unrelated to the template, mostly averages out. Among all filters, the matched filter gives the *highest possible peak SNR* for a known pulse in white noise — which is why it's the heart of radar and digital-comms receivers.

A related trick is **integration**: average many repeats of the same signal. The signal repeats and reinforces, while the random noise partly cancels itself, so the SNR climbs the more repeats you add. That's how a faint, noisy radar echo or a weak star in a long-exposure photo gradually emerges from the hiss.

**Recap.** SNR is the signal-to-noise power ratio and governs detectability; filtering removes out-of-band noise; the matched filter correlates against a known signal shape to maximize peak SNR; averaging many repeats also lifts the signal out of the noise.

items.prompt: Signal-to-noise ratio (SNR) is:
choices: The signal power divided by the noise power
The signal power minus the temperature
The total power of signal plus noise
The frequency of the signal divided by its bandwidth
items.explanation: SNR is a ratio: signal power over noise power (often expressed in decibels). It measures how far the signal stands above the noise. It is not a sum of powers, nor a frequency ratio, nor anything involving temperature directly.

items.prompt: Why doesn't simply turning up the volume (amplifying everything) improve detection?
choices: Because amplifiers cannot increase signal power
Because it boosts the signal and the noise by the same factor, leaving the SNR unchanged
Because volume only affects frequency, not power
Because louder signals are always harder to detect
items.explanation: Amplifying scales the signal and the noise together, so their ratio — the SNR — stays the same. Since detection depends on SNR, not absolute level, you gain nothing. This is the whisper-at-a-rock-concert problem: making everything louder doesn't separate the whisper from the roar.

items.prompt: A higher SNR means:
choices: The signal is harder to tell apart from the noise
The signal stands out more clearly above the noise floor and is easier to detect
There is more noise than signal
The signal has disappeared
items.explanation: A bigger signal-to-noise ratio means the signal sticks up further above the noise floor, so it's easier to spot and decode. More noise than signal would be a *low* SNR (below 1, or negative in dB), the opposite case.

items.prompt: How can a filter improve SNR when the signal is in a narrow band but noise is spread everywhere?
choices: By amplifying both signal and noise equally
By keeping only the signal's frequency band and rejecting noise outside it
By adding more noise to swamp the signal
By converting the signal into noise
items.explanation: If the signal occupies a narrow band but white noise is everywhere, a filter that passes only that band keeps the signal while discarding the out-of-band noise — less noise, same signal, higher SNR. Amplifying both equally (A) wouldn't change the ratio.

items.prompt: When white noise passes through a filter, the output noise is generally:
choices: Still perfectly white (flat spectrum)
Colored, because the filter reshapes the noise's spectrum
Completely removed
Turned into a pure tone
items.explanation: A filter passes some frequencies more than others, so it reshapes the once-flat (white) spectrum into an uneven one — the output noise is now colored. The filter reduces noise but can't remove it entirely, and it doesn't create a pure tone from random noise.

items.prompt: A matched filter is designed to:
choices: Pass every frequency equally
Correlate the incoming data against a stored copy of the known signal shape, maximizing peak SNR
Add noise that matches the signal
Remove the signal and keep the noise
items.explanation: A matched filter is shaped like the expected signal and effectively correlates the received data with that template. When the real signal lines up, the output spikes; unrelated noise mostly averages out. For a known pulse in white noise, it gives the best possible peak SNR of any filter.

items.prompt: What must you know in advance to use a matched filter?
choices: The exact future values of the noise
The shape of the signal you are looking for
The room temperature
Nothing at all — it works on any unknown signal equally well
items.explanation: A matched filter is matched to a *known* signal shape — you need a template of what you're hunting for so the filter can correlate against it. You do not need (and cannot have) the future noise values, and it is specifically tuned to a known shape, not a blind catch-all.

items.prompt: Why does the matched filter make the SIGNAL spike while NOISE mostly averages out?
choices: Because the noise is also shaped exactly like the template
Because the signal lines up (correlates) strongly with the stored template, while random noise does not
Because the filter deletes the noise samples one by one
Because noise has more power than the signal
items.explanation: When the real signal arrives, it matches the template point-for-point, so the correlation adds up to a large peak. Noise is unrelated to the template, so its contributions are just as likely to add as to cancel and they largely wash out. If the noise matched the template (A), it wouldn't be random noise.

items.prompt: Averaging (integrating) many repeats of the same signal raises SNR because:
choices: The signal cancels itself while noise reinforces
The signal reinforces (it repeats), while the random noise partly cancels itself
It increases the noise faster than the signal
It changes the signal's frequency
items.explanation: The signal is the same each repeat, so summing the repeats reinforces it. The noise is random and different each time, so its ups and downs partly cancel when added. The signal therefore grows faster than the noise, lifting the SNR — the basis of long-exposure astrophotography and radar pulse integration.

items.prompt: A long-exposure photo of a faint star pulls it out of the sensor noise. This is an everyday example of:
choices: Reducing SNR on purpose
Integration / averaging lifting a repeated signal above random noise
Turning the signal into white noise
A matched filter for radio waves
items.explanation: A long exposure keeps collecting the steady starlight (the repeated signal) while the random sensor noise partly averages out — the same integration idea that lets radar sum many echoes. It raises SNR (so A is backwards), and it isn't a matched filter, which instead correlates against a known signal *shape*.

items.prompt: Putting it together: which approach does NOT help you detect a weak signal hidden in noise?
choices: Filtering out frequency bands where only noise lives
Using a matched filter tuned to the known signal shape
Averaging many repeats of the signal
Amplifying the signal and noise together by the same factor
items.explanation: Amplifying everything equally scales signal and noise by the same factor, leaving SNR unchanged — so it doesn't help detection. The other three all raise SNR: band filtering removes out-of-band noise, a matched filter maximizes peak SNR for a known shape, and averaging lets the repeated signal grow while noise partly cancels.
